text: Nine weeks of plyometric training (3x30min sessions/week) improves running economy at high speeds (18 km/h) by approximately 4.1% in highly trained distance runners.
Plyometric training improves running economy at higher speeds but not at lower speeds (no significant improvement at 14 or 16 km/h), suggesting benefits are velocity-dependent.
specific_value: significant at 18 km/h but not at 14 or 16 km/h

text: Plyometric training does not significantly change VO2max or cardiorespiratory measures in highly trained runners, indicating running economy improvements are neuromuscular rather than cardiovascular.
The mechanism of plyometric-induced running economy improvement likely resides in muscular adaptations (e.g., improved muscle-tendon stiffness, elastic energy storage) or improved running mechanics.
A plyometric training dose of 3 sessions per week, 30 minutes per session, for 9 weeks is sufficient to produce measurable running economy improvements when added to normal distance training.
Plyometric training trends toward improving muscle power output (~15% increase in average power during 5-jump test) and rate of force development (~14% faster time to maximal dynamic strength).
